food_subgroup: Noodle dish — stir-fried or soupy tapioca-based noodles

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Lou Xu Fun / rat tail noodles. FOODON_03315595 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (noodle-based dishes) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for tapioca rat-tail noodle dishes in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Lou Xu Fun or rat tail noodles. SNOMED CT does not cover this specific regional dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Lou Xu Fun in Singapore is approximately 350 g, comprising a bowl or plate of tapioca noodles with protein, vegetables, and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Noodles (Mixed) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Lou Xu Fun (rat tail noodles) is a tapioca-starch noodle dish closely analogous to fried noodle dishes in the HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo 'Fried Noodles (Mixed)' entry, which represents a stir-fried noodle dish with protein and vegetables — the closest approved primary source match for this Singaporean Chinese dish. Values are per 100 g of prepared dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles and tapioca-based noodle analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lou Xu Fun (rat tail noodles / lao shu fen). The noodles are made from tapioca starch, which has a GI profile similar to rice noodles. Rice noodles have a published GI of approximately 58 (medium) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI tables. This value is used as the closest defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 61.25) / 100 ≈ 35.5, rounded to 35.6. Serving carbohydrate = 17.5 g/100g × 350 g = 61.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Lou Xu Fun prepared with soy sauce and seasoning contributes approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1,470 mg per standard 350 g serving), which represents a substantial portion of the 2,000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sodium preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index tapioca noodles with moderate carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lou Xu Fun has an estimated medium GI of approximately 58 based on its tapioca-starch noodle base, and a glycemic load of approximately 35.6 per standard serving, which is high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a standard serving and pair with non-starchy vegetables and lean protein to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from lard or oil and elevated sodium from seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lou Xu Fun contains approximately 1.5 g saturated fat per 100 g and high sodium from soy-based sauces; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose versions prepared with vegetable oil rather than lard, request reduced-sodium seasoning, and limit portion to reduce overall saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lou Xu Fun (老鼠粉, literally 'rat tail noodles' or 'mouse tail noodles') is a popular Singaporean Chinese noodle dish made from short, tapered noodles crafted from tapioca starch and rice flour. The noodles are named for their distinctive shape — thick in the middle and tapering to a point at each end, resembling a rat's tail. They are also known as Silver Needle Noodles or Bee Tai Mak in the Hokkien dialect. The dish is commonly served stir-fried (dry) or in a light soup broth, typically with ingredients such as pork slices, prawns, bean sprouts, spring onions, and seasoned with soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sesame oil, and garlic. The noodles have a smooth, slightly chewy, and slippery texture with a mild, neutral flavour that absorbs surrounding sauces well. Lou Xu Fun is a staple comfort food in Singapore's hawker centres and coffee shops, with roots in Hakka and Teochew Chinese culinary traditions. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish providing carbohydrates primarily from tapioca starch, with protein from meat or seafood additions. The sodium content is elevated due to soy-based seasoning, making it a concern for hypertensive individuals. The medium glycemic index of the tapioca noodle base (estimated GI ~58) and high glycemic load per serving make portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is dairy-free and generally not suitable for vegetarians or vegans in its standard preparation due to meat or seafood content.
common_user_queries: What is lao shu fen made of?
Is Lou Xu Fun gluten free?
Is Bee Tai Mak the same as Lou Xu Fun?
Is Lou Xu Fun suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of tapioca noodles?

confidence_reason: Lou Xu Fun is a recognised Singaporean Chinese dish but no exact nutrient entry exists in HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN for this specific noodle type. Values are adapted from the closest HPB Foodo fried noodle (mixed) category, which is the highest-priority approved source available. GI is estimated from the analogous rice/tapioca noodle GI published in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo 'Fried Noodles (Mixed)' as the closest approved primary source; no exact Lou Xu Fun entry was found in HPB Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases as of the access date. monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry for this food category. polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. trans_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. potassium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. calcium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. iron_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. cholesterol_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. vitamin_c_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. water_g is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. GI estimated from analogous tapioca/rice noodle GI (~58) per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Lou Xu Fun was found. Halal status is not confirmed as pork is a common ingredient in traditional preparations. The dish served at Hai Kah Lang (Funan Mall) on GrabFood Singapore at SGD 4.82 is the seed restaurant reference.
